dominantFinding: Chhattisgarh's 60% rainfed agriculture and ≥8 lakh ha of degraded commons are structurally underutilised — the lone SOC scheme (Rainfed Area Development) proves watershed commons can deliver ₹7,000–9,000/HH/year, yet nine sibling schemes ignore this blueprint entirely.
dominantFailure: Agriculture Department schemes overwhelmingly fail T1 (Commons Resource) and T4 (Sustainability Lock). 90% of schemes are oriented toward individual farm subsidies on private landholdings without formalized commons resource governance. Even where schemes target water harvesting (commons potential), they lack mandatory replenishment rules and post-project monitoring.

commonsProfile.keyEcologicalContext: 70% of Chhattisgarh's blocks show groundwater stress; ≈60% of agriculture is rainfed — water-harvesting commons is the primary leverage point for SOC transition

crossCuttingPriorities: Establish Gram Commons Governance Framework (cost ₹8-12 Cr, timeline 18-24 months)
Implement Extraction/Replenishment Monitoring System (cost ₹5-7 Cr, timeline 12-18 months)
Design O&M Fund Mechanism for All Scheme Assets (reallocation, no new budget)
Pilot Commons-Based Agricultural Development Model in 100 Gram Sabhas (cost ₹50-80 Cr, 24-month pilot)
contextualFactors: Agricultural Commons Potential: ≥8 lakh hectares degraded pasturelands and over-exploited groundwater aquifers (70% blocks under stress)
Tribal & SC Population Advantage: 32% ST, ≥20% SC — traditional commons governance practices have deeper institutional memory
Rainfed Agriculture Dominance: ≈60% of CG agriculture is rainfed, precisely matched to Rainfed Area Development (the full-SOC scheme)
Gram Sabha Empowerment Precedent: Suraji Gaon Yojana has established gram sabha-led commons management framework covering ≈4,000+ gram sabhas

schemes.name: Kisan Samagra Vikas Yojana
schemes.objective: Provide integrated support to farmers across crop production, soil health, irrigation, and input subsidies targeting overall agricultural productivity enhancement on individual private landholdings.
governanceFlags: MISSING: T1.5 — No recognition of indigenous or customary commons practices
MISSING: T2.4 — No gram sabha commons deliberation mechanism
MISSING: T3.5 — No systematic per-household income outcome tracking
MISSING: T4.6 — No community-driven governance or oversight mechanism
CLIMATE-RISK: T4.7 — Groundwater stress trigger active; T4.3 threshold elevated to ≥2

T1_1.note: Scheme targets private farm landholdings exclusively; no commons resource identified in scheme objectives or implementation guidelines. All subsidies flow to individual landholders.

T1_2.note: No community access right established; individual farmer subsidy model with no collective governance structure or commons entitlement of any kind.

T1_3.note: Estimated <5% of scheme expenditure directed toward commons assets (soil health demonstration plots); remainder funds individual input subsidies for seeds, fertilizers, and pesticides.

T1_4.note: All scheme outputs (seeds, fertilizers, equipment) are excludable private goods allocated to individual beneficiaries; no community benefit-sharing or non-excludable resource created.

T1_5.flag: No recognition of indigenous or customary commons practices
T1_5.note: Scheme design is purely productivity-enhancement framing; no acknowledgment of traditional village commons, indigenous seed sovereignty, or customary access rights.

T1_6.note: No legal or policy mandate to target commons resources; scheme documentation explicitly targets private landholding farmers with individual subsidy disbursement.

T2_1.note: Locality fraction estimated at 0.60; beneficiaries dispersed across all blocks without commons-locality clustering or spatial concentration requirement. Below 0.70 threshold.

T2_2.note: SC/ST share approximately 20%; no explicit SC/ST priority mandate in scheme guidelines. CG has 44% ST+SC population but scheme does not target this demographic. Below 0.30 threshold.

T2_3.note: No gram sabha mandate; implementation through individual farmer registration and block-level agriculture department administration without community oversight.

T2_4.flag: No gram sabha commons deliberation mechanism
T2_4.note: Gram sabha not involved in scheme targeting, resource allocation, or benefit distribution decisions; purely bureaucratic channel.

T2_5.flag: CG GP Act commons provisions not activated
T2_5.note: No linkage to Chhattisgarh Gram Panchayat Act commons management or resource planning provisions.

T3_1.note: No mandated livelihood output; scheme delivers input subsidies without guaranteed productivity outcome or income mandate for beneficiaries.

T3_2.note: Projected income increment ₹2,000–3,000/HH/year from improved inputs on private land; midpoint ₹2,500 below the ₹3,000 observed threshold.

T3_3.note: Single input subsidy channel; no diversified livelihood income pathways defined or monitored under the scheme.

T3_4.note: No institutional offtake mechanism; outputs sold at open market without guaranteed procurement or MSP linkage for scheme beneficiaries.

T3_5.flag: No systematic per-household income outcome tracking
T3_5.note: Monitoring limited to input distribution counts and beneficiary registration; no income outcome tracking per household.

T4_1.note: No replenishment rule for soil, groundwater, or any natural resource; scheme provides inputs without sustainability conditionality or resource recovery obligation.

T4_2.note: Extraction/replenishment ratio undefined and unmeasured; no groundwater monitoring or soil depletion tracking framework exists in scheme design.

T4_3.note: No monitoring framework of any kind. CG groundwater stress context requires score ≥2; current score is 0 — scheme has zero sustainability monitoring.

T4_4.note: No penalty mechanism for resource degradation, non-compliance, or sustainability violations; scheme has no conditionality linked to ecological outcomes.

T4_5.note: No O&M fund or long-term stewardship mechanism; no provision for maintenance of any scheme-created assets or resource systems.

T4_6.flag: No community-driven governance or oversight mechanism
T4_6.note: Scheme administered top-down through agriculture department hierarchy; no community ownership, accountability, or participation structure.

T4_7.note: Chhattisgarh: 70% of blocks are groundwater over-stressed per CGWB data. T4.3 pass threshold elevated from ≥1 to ≥2 for all agriculture schemes in the state.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (private landholding); T1.2=0 (no community access right); T1.3~0.05 (expenditure on commons <5%); T1.4=0 (individual farmer owns outputs)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.60 (locality fraction: dispersed across blocks); T2.2: SC/ST priority absent; T2.3=0 (no gram sabha mandate)
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (no livelihood output mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.2,000-3,000/year (below threshold); T3.3=0 (single crop pathway); T3.4=0 (no institutional offtake)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0 (no replenishment rule); T4.2: E/R undefined; T4.3=0 (no monitoring); T4.4=0; T4.5=0

upgradePathway: Reorient to Commons Water Harvesting Base: Mandate ≥40% of scheme expenditure on village-level water harvesting commons (check-dams, farm ponds, groundwater recharge structures)
Commons Governance Mandate: Link subsidy access to mandatory participation in gram-level commons management committee
Sustainability Lock: Establish extraction/replenishment monitoring (E/R ratio ≤1.2 for groundwater); mandatory 10% of annual yield reinvested in aquifer recharge
Income Threshold: Target ₹5,000+ annual income increment through groundwater commons access (shared irrigation, reduced extraction costs)

references.title: Kisan Samagra Vikas Yojana — Official Scheme Guidelines, Agriculture Department CG
Chhattisgarh Agriculture Department — Budget Allocation 2024-25

schemes.name: Rajiv Gandhi Kisan Nyay Yojana
schemes.objective: Provide direct benefit transfer to farmers as compensation for commodity price fluctuations and crop loss, supporting income stability and household food security.
governanceFlags: MISSING: T1.5 — No recognition of indigenous or customary commons practices
MISSING: T2.3 — No gram sabha mandate; DBT disbursement bypasses community governance
MISSING: T2.4 — No gram sabha commons deliberation
MISSING: T3.5 — No commons-linked income outcome tracking
MISSING: T4.6 — No community-driven governance mechanism
CLIMATE-RISK: T4.7 — Groundwater stress trigger active

T1_1.note: DBT scheme for private landholding farmers; no commons resource involved in design or delivery. Cash transfer does not engage any collective resource.

T1_2.note: No community access right; scheme is an individual income support transfer without any commons entitlement or collective resource governance.

T1_3.note: Zero expenditure on commons; entire scheme budget is direct cash transfer to individual farmer bank accounts with no commons investment component.

T1_4.note: Cash transfer is a purely private good — excludable and rivalrous benefit; no community benefit or non-excludable resource created.

T1_5.flag: No recognition of indigenous or customary commons practices
T1_5.note: DBT framing has no connection to traditional commons institutions, indigenous farming practices, or customary land access systems.

T1_6.note: No legal mandate to target commons; scheme is income compensation mechanism with no commons resource orientation in enabling legislation.

T2_1.note: Locality fraction approximately 0.75; scheme targets village-resident registered farmers with reasonably high geographic concentration in agricultural villages.

T2_2.note: SC/ST share approximately 42% of beneficiaries, reflecting CG demographic composition; above 0.30 threshold for dweller inclusion.

T2_3.note: No gram sabha mandate; DBT delivered directly to farmer bank accounts via individual registration — bypasses community governance entirely.

T2_4.note: Gram sabha has no role in targeting, eligibility, or distribution decisions; scheme is purely administrative channel.

T2_5.flag: CG GP Act commons provisions not activated
T2_5.note: No linkage to CG Gram Panchayat Act commons management provisions or community resource governance.

T3_1.note: No mandated livelihood output from commons engagement; cash transfer supports income floor but does not mandate productive commons-based livelihood activity.

T3_2.note: Observed per-farmer cash transfer ₹1,500–2,500/year; midpoint ₹2,000 below the ₹3,000 observed commons income threshold. Transfer is compensation, not commons-derived income.

T3_3.note: Single cash disbursement channel; no livelihood pathway diversification; income is a fixed government transfer not linked to productive commons engagement.

T3_4.note: No institutional offtake; cash transfer does not create any market linkage or value-chain integration for farmer produce.

T3_5.note: Monitoring tracks only DBT disbursement completion; no tracking of income enhancement from commons engagement or livelihood outcomes.

T4_1.note: No replenishment rule; DBT scheme has no connection to natural resource extraction or sustainability; N/A by design but scheme also does not incentivize conservation.

T4_2.note: No extraction/replenishment framework; scheme does not engage any subtractable resource, so no rho measurement possible or defined.

T4_3.note: No monitoring framework; scheme does not track any natural resource metric. CG groundwater stress elevates threshold to ≥2 which scheme scores 0.

T4_4.note: No penalty mechanism for resource degradation; DBT design has no conditionality linked to ecological or commons sustainability outcomes.

T4_5.note: No O&M fund or stewardship mechanism; DBT disbursements create no lasting resource asset requiring maintenance.

T4_6.note: No community institution involved in governance, oversight, or accountability; scheme is fully centralized DBT delivery.

T4_7.note: CG context: 70% blocks groundwater stressed. Scheme does not address groundwater but stress trigger applies to all agriculture schemes in the state, elevating T4.3 threshold.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (private landholding); T1.2=0 (no commons resource involved); T1.3~0.00 (no expenditure on commons); T1.4=0
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.75 (locality fraction); T2.2~0.42 (SC/ST share); T2.3=0 (no gram sabha mandate)
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (no livelihood output mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.1,500-2,500/year; T3.3=0; T3.4=0
evidence.t4: T4.1=0 (no replenishment rule); T4.2: N/A; T4.3=0; T4.4=0; T4.5=0

upgradePathway: Link to Commons Water Harvesting: Condition cash transfer on farmer participation in village commons water harvesting committees
Livelihood Productivity Linkage: Transform DBT from subsistence transfer to livelihood productivity transfer pegged to commons groundwater access
Gram Commons Governance: Require gram sabha establishment of farmer commons committee as prerequisite for block-level disbursement
Sustainability Commitment: Link transfer continuation to farmer adoption of soil/water conservation and commons maintenance

references.title: Rajiv Gandhi Kisan Nyay Yojana — Official Portal, Agriculture Dept CG
Rajiv Gandhi Kisan Nyay Yojana — ₹6,000 Cr DBT Disbursement Update, PIB

schemes.name: Suraji Gaon Yojana
schemes.nodalMinistry: Agriculture & Panchayat and Rural Development, Chhattisgarh
schemes.objective: Integrated rural commons development through four strategic commons interventions: Narwa (water conservation), Garwa (pasture/grazing commons), Ghurva (livestock/fodder), and Bari (kitchen gardens) with community-based resource management.
governanceFlags: STRUCTURAL: T4.1 — No binding replenishment rule; E/R ratio undefined and unmonitored for Narwa water commons
STRUCTURAL: T4.2 — Groundwater extraction/replenishment ratio not measured across Narwa sites
STRUCTURAL: T4.3 — Periodic monitoring exists but score 1 below CG climate-stress threshold of ≥2
CLIMATE-RISK: T4.7 — Groundwater stress trigger active; requires real-time monitoring and E/R ≤1.25

T1_1.note: Scheme explicitly targets village commons: Narwa (streams/water commons), Garwa (gauchars/grazing commons), Ghurva (composting grounds), Bari (kitchen garden commons). Core commons typology.

T1_2.note: Community access right formalized through gram sabha resolution and Gram Panchayat mandate; access to Narwa water commons and Garwa grazing formalized but not statutory under a central act.

T1_3.note: Approximately 75% of scheme expenditure directed to commons infrastructure (Narwa water harvesting, Garwa fencing and restoration, Ghurva composting units); well above 50% threshold.

T1_4.note: Commons outputs (groundwater recharge, pasture biomass, watershed soil moisture) are non-excludable village-wide benefits; entire gram panchayat benefits from commons restoration.

T1_5.note: Scheme explicitly builds on traditional Narwa water harvesting and Garwa grazing practices rooted in Chhattisgarh village commons culture; indigenous knowledge integrated in design.

T1_6.note: CG Government Cabinet resolution authorizes Suraji Gaon as state commons development policy; Agriculture and PRD departments jointly mandated to implement commons governance.

T2_1.note: Locality fraction approximately 0.85; scheme targets gram panchayat as unit — all resident households benefit from commons restoration. High spatial concentration.

T2_2.note: SC/ST share approximately 50% of direct beneficiaries, reflecting CG tribal belt coverage; ST/SC priority explicitly mentioned in gram sabha governance mandate.

T2_3.note: Gram sabha governance mandated for all four commons interventions; 4,000+ gram sabhas have active Narwa-Garwa committees with quarterly review meetings.

T2_4.note: Gram sabha formally deliberates on commons development plans, annual work calendars, and resource allocation for all four Suraji Gaon components; documented in gram sabha proceedings.

T2_5.note: CG Gram Panchayat Act commons provisions activated; gram panchayats receive dedicated funds through State Finance Commission for commons maintenance under Suraji Gaon.

T3_1.note: Livelihood income mandate explicit: Narwa increases irrigation access, Garwa improves fodder and dairy income, Bari enables vegetable income. Scheme targets quantifiable household income improvement.

T3_2.note: Observed income increment ₹6,000–8,000/HH/year across pilot gram panchayats from combined Narwa (irrigation savings), Garwa (fodder value), and Bari (vegetable income); midpoint ₹7,000 well above ₹3,000 threshold.

pathways: Narwa — groundwater recharge enabling rain-fed crop yield improvement
Garwa — pasture restoration enabling dairy cattle fodder income
Ghurva — composting reducing input cost and enabling organic produce premium
Bari — kitchen garden enabling vegetable self-sufficiency and sale
T3_3.note: Multiple livelihood income pathways across four commons components; minimum two measurable pathways (Narwa + Garwa) in all implementing gram panchayats.

T3_4.offtakeMechanism: Gram cooperative procurement + CGMF linkage for organic produce; direct consumption and local market for kitchen garden output
T3_4.note: Institutional offtake through gram cooperative linkage for Garwa dairy produce; Bari produce consumed directly or sold locally; Narwa benefits captured as irrigation savings.

T3_5.flag: No systematic per-household income outcome tracking across all four components
T3_5.note: Monitoring tracks physical outputs (check-dams built, area fenced) but lacks systematic household-level income attribution across all Narwa-Garwa-Ghurva-Bari components.

T4_1.note: No binding replenishment rule codified; Narwa water harvesting builds recharge infrastructure but no E/R limit mandate exists. Partial intent without enforceable rule — maps to score 0 in new binary framework.

T4_2.note: Groundwater extraction/replenishment ratio not defined or measured at any Narwa site; CGWB data available at district level but not integrated into scheme monitoring.

T4_3.note: Periodic monitoring of physical infrastructure exists (score 1); however CG groundwater stress context requires score ≥2 (outcome monitoring with annual audit). Current monitoring is activity-based not outcome-based.

T4_4.note: No penalty mechanism for resource over-extraction or commons degradation; gram sabha review process is advisory with no financial conditionality linked to ecological outcomes.

T4_5.note: No dedicated O&M fund for Narwa/Garwa infrastructure maintenance; gram panchayat general fund used informally; no mandatory maintenance allocation from scheme budget.

T4_6.note: Community-driven governance through gram sabha commons committees is a defining feature of Suraji Gaon; Narwa-Garwa Samitis are functional community institutions for commons management.

T4_7.note: CG: 70% blocks groundwater stressed per CGWB data; Narwa water commons directly address groundwater recharge but T4.3 monitoring must reach score ≥2 given stress context.

evidence.t1: T1.1=B (village commons); T1.2=1 (community access formalized); T1.3~0.75 (expenditure on commons); T1.4=2 (non-excludable)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.85 (locality fraction); T2.2~0.50 (SC/ST share); T2.3=1 (gram sabha governance mandated)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (livelihood output mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.6,000-8,000/HH/year; T3.3=2 (multiple pathways); T3.4=1 (institutional offtake)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0.5 (partial replenishment); T4.2: undefined; T4.3=0.5 (periodic monitoring); T4.4=0; T4.5=0

upgradePathway: Mandatory Replenishment Rule: Codify E/R ≤1.25 for groundwater extraction with shortfall triggering mandatory dry-season recharge (Atal Jal water budgeting model)
Community Monitoring System: Establish Gram Water Committee conducting quarterly monitoring — estimated cost ₹3-5 Cr, timeline 12-18 months
Real-Time Monitoring Platform: Deploy mobile app for extraction/recharge tracking linked to gram panchayat dashboard
O&M Fund Mechanism: Mandate 10% of scheme capital as dedicated O&M reserve fund held in community cooperative account

references.title: Suraji Gaon Yojana — Government of Chhattisgarh Official Programme Page
Narwa-Garwa-Ghurva-Bari Programme Evaluation — Implementation Review, CG Agriculture
Central Ground Water Board — Groundwater Year Book, Chhattisgarh (Block-level Stress Assessment)

schemes.name: Godhan Nyay Yojana
schemes.reach: 35 lakh cattle holders; 10,000+ Gauthan (village commons) sites
schemes.objective: Procure cow dung from farmers/grazers at guaranteed prices (₹2/kg), supporting organic manure production and farmer income while incentivizing cattle rearing for commons grazing.
governanceFlags: STRUCTURAL: T4.1 — No binding grazing carrying-capacity rule; pasture overuse risk unmitigated
STRUCTURAL: T4.2 — Pasture extraction/replenishment ratio (stocking rate vs biomass) undefined
STRUCTURAL: T4.3 — No systematic pasture monitoring; zero outcome-based monitoring score
CLIMATE-RISK: T4.7 — Groundwater stress trigger active; Gauthan water commons also at risk

T1_1.note: Scheme explicitly targets community gauchars (grazing commons) and Gauthan (village livestock commons areas); cow dung is sourced from cattle grazed on shared village pastures.

T1_2.note: Community access right formalized through gram sabha Gauthan committee; grazing commons access is established practice with state recognition, though not statutory under a central act.

T1_3.note: Approximately 65% of scheme expenditure flows to commons-linked activities: Gauthan development, pasture restoration, cow dung procurement from commons grazers; above 50% threshold.

T1_4.note: Partial non-excludability: grazing commons benefits (pasture, water, manure from commons cattle) are accessible to all gram panchayat livestock holders, though procurement benefits concentrate on active grazers.

T1_5.note: Scheme is grounded in traditional Chhattisgarh cattle commons culture (Gauthan system); cow dung procurement explicitly leverages indigenous cattle-rearing and gazing commons practice.

T1_6.note: Chhattisgarh Godhan Nyay Yojana enacted with dedicated state policy mandate; Gauthan committee legally constituted under gram panchayat framework with explicit commons management role.

T2_1.note: Locality fraction approximately 0.80; scheme targets cattle-rearing residents of gram panchayat; Gauthan sites are village commons with high community co-location.

T2_2.note: SC/ST share approximately 45% of Gauthan beneficiaries; tribal and SC communities are primary cattle grazers in CG's gauchars; explicit ST community targeting in Gauthan design.

T2_3.note: Gram sabha Gauthan management committee mandated and operational; 10,000+ Gauthan committees actively managing procurement, composting, and pasture governance.

T2_4.note: Gram sabha deliberates on Gauthan development plans, pasture management rules, and procurement eligibility; documented in gram sabha resolutions at implementation sites.

T2_5.note: CG GP Act commons management provisions activated for Gauthan sites; gram panchayat has legal authority over gauchars under CG MP Act and Godhan Nyay enabling order.

T3_1.note: Income mandate explicit: ₹2/kg guaranteed procurement price for cow dung creates a direct, measurable income stream for cattle-owning rural households from commons grazing.

T3_2.note: Observed income increment ₹4,000–6,000/HH/year from cow dung procurement at ₹2/kg; for average 5-6 cattle household generating 15-20 kg/day; midpoint ₹5,000 above ₹3,000 threshold.

pathways: Cow dung procurement income at ₹2/kg guaranteed price
Vermicompost and organic manure income from Gauthan processing units
T3_3.note: Primary pathway is cow dung procurement income; secondary pathway emerging from Gauthan vermicompost sales to farmers. Single-primary pathway with one emergent secondary.

T3_4.offtakeMechanism: State procurement at guaranteed price ₹2/kg via CGMF and Gauthan cooperatives; vermicompost sold to Agriculture Dept at fixed rate
T3_4.note: Institutional offtake guaranteed through state procurement at fixed price; Chhattisgarh Mineral Foundation (CGMF) manages procurement and payment to Gauthan committees.

T3_5.flag: No systematic per-household income attribution across Gauthan and pasture components
T3_5.note: Procurement amounts tracked per Gauthan but household-level income attribution and comparison to baseline is not systematically measured across all sites.

T4_1.note: No binding carrying-capacity rule for gauchars; no stocking rate limit (animals per hectare) defined or enforced. Partial intent to manage pastures exists but no codified replenishment mandate — maps to score 0.

T4_2.note: Pasture extraction/replenishment ratio (stocking rate vs biomass regeneration rate) undefined and unmeasured; no gazing intensity monitoring framework linked to procurement eligibility.

T4_3.note: No systematic pasture monitoring framework; physical outputs tracked (Gauthan sheds built, dung procured) but no vegetation cover, soil quality, or pasture health metrics. CG stress requires ≥2; score is 0.

T4_4.note: No penalty mechanism for pasture overuse or commons degradation; procurement continues regardless of pasture health outcomes; no financial conditionality on ecological condition.

T4_5.note: No dedicated pasture O&M fund; Gauthan infrastructure (sheds, vermicompost units) maintained ad hoc; no mandatory maintenance allocation from procurement revenue.

T4_6.note: Gauthan management committees are a genuine community institution with governance authority over commons; community-driven design is a structural feature of Godhan Nyay.

T4_7.note: CG 70% blocks groundwater stressed; Gauthan sites also depend on water commons for cattle; stress trigger elevates T4.3 threshold to ≥2 for outcome monitoring.

evidence.t1: T1.1=D (community grazing commons); T1.2=1 (access right formalized); T1.3~0.65 (expenditure on commons); T1.4=1 (partial non-excludability)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.80 (locality fraction); T2.2~0.45 (SC/ST share); T2.3=1 (gram sabha governance)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (income indicator mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.4,000-6,000/HH/year; T3.3=1 (single pathway: manure income); T3.4=1 (institutional offtake via cooperatives)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0.5 (partial — no binding grazing limit); T4.2: undefined; T4.3=0 (no monitoring framework); T4.4=0; T4.5=0.5

upgradePathway: Sustainable Cattle Carrying Capacity: Link procurement eligibility to gram-level pasture sustainability assessment with max 0.8 animals/hectare (Amrit Sarovar user group model)
Grazing Commons Monitoring: Integrate with Suraji Gaon pasture monitoring; quarterly vegetation assessments
Replenishment Obligation: Mandatory 5% of Godhan procurement revenue to pasture restoration — estimated cost ₹2-3 Cr
Penalty Mechanism: Withhold 25% of monthly payments if quarterly monitoring shows pasture degradation >15%

references.title: Godhan Nyay Yojana — Official Scheme Page, Agriculture Department CG
Godhan Nyay Yojana — Third-Party Evaluation Report, Chhattisgarh State
Godhan Nyay Yojana — Budget Allocation and Disbursement Data 2023-24

schemes.name: Krishi Yantrikikarana (Farm Mechanization)
schemes.reach: Individual landholding farmers across CG districts
schemes.objective: Provide subsidies (₹50,000-100,000 per unit) to individual farmers for purchase of agricultural machinery (tractors, threshers, harvesters), reducing labor costs and increasing operational efficiency.
governanceFlags: MISSING: T1.5 — No recognition of indigenous commons or traditional shared-equipment practices
MISSING: T2.4 — No gram sabha involvement in equipment allocation or commons governance
MISSING: T3.5 — No income outcome tracking beyond equipment distribution counts
MISSING: T4.6 — No community-driven governance; purely individual subsidy scheme
CLIMATE-RISK: T4.7 — Groundwater stress trigger active

T1_1.note: Scheme subsidizes individual purchase of agricultural equipment (tractors, threshers) on private land; no commons resource involved. Equipment is private property of subsidy recipient.

T1_2.note: No community access right; individual equipment ownership with no commons-use obligation; equipment subsidy creates private asset, not commons asset.

T1_3.note: Negligible commons expenditure (<2%); virtually entire budget subsidizes individual equipment purchase with no commons infrastructure or resource investment component.

T1_4.note: Equipment (tractors, threshers) is fully excludable private good; subsidy recipient owns and controls access; no community benefit or non-excludable output created.

T1_5.flag: No recognition of indigenous commons or traditional shared-equipment practices
T1_5.note: Scheme ignores traditional shared-equipment arrangements (community tractors, cooperative threshing) common in CG villages; purely individual subsidy framing.

T1_6.note: No legal or policy mandate for commons orientation; scheme is individual equipment subsidy with no commons resource governance requirement.

T2_1.note: Locality fraction approximately 0.50; beneficiaries dispersed across state by individual application; no spatial clustering or commons-locality requirement. Below 0.70 threshold.

T2_2.note: SC/ST share approximately 20%; no explicit SC/ST priority in equipment subsidy guidelines. General farmer category dominates application pool given financial capacity requirements. Below 0.30 threshold.

T2_3.note: No gram sabha mandate; individual application process through district agriculture office without community governance role.

T2_4.flag: No gram sabha involvement in equipment allocation or commons governance
T2_4.note: Gram sabha has no role in targeting, allocation, or oversight of equipment subsidies; purely administrative individual application.

T2_5.flag: CG GP Act commons provisions not activated
T2_5.note: No linkage to gram panchayat commons provisions; equipment remains individual asset outside panchayat commons framework.

T3_1.note: No mandated livelihood output from commons engagement; equipment subsidy reduces labor cost but does not mandate any commons-based productivity outcome or income commitment.

T3_2.note: Projected income increment ₹1,000–2,000/HH/year from labor savings and yield improvement from mechanization; midpoint ₹1,500 well below ₹3,000 threshold.

T3_3.note: Single input (labor-saving) channel; no diversified livelihood pathways; benefit is limited to reduced production cost on private landholding.

T3_4.note: No institutional offtake; equipment benefit is cost reduction on individual farm; no market linkage or value chain integration.

T3_5.flag: No income outcome tracking beyond equipment distribution counts
T3_5.note: Monitoring tracks equipment subsidies disbursed and equipment types, not actual income improvement or livelihood outcomes for beneficiaries.

T4_1.note: No replenishment rule; mechanization subsidy has no sustainability conditionality for soil, water, or any natural resource; no ecological obligation attached to subsidy.

T4_2.note: No extraction/replenishment framework applicable; scheme does not engage any measurable commons resource; rho undefined and irrelevant to current scheme design.

T4_3.note: No monitoring framework; scheme tracks equipment distribution only. CG groundwater stress requires score ≥2; scheme scores 0 on any resource monitoring metric.

T4_4.note: No penalty mechanism; equipment subsidy disbursed unconditionally with no sustainability or commons-use conditionality.

T4_5.note: No O&M fund; individual equipment maintenance is responsibility of subsidy recipient without any state-supported maintenance mechanism.

T4_6.flag: No community-driven governance; purely individual subsidy scheme
T4_6.note: No community institution involved; purely individual application and ownership model with no community accountability.

T4_7.note: CG 70% blocks groundwater stressed; mechanization (especially groundwater-dependent irrigation mechanization) could accelerate extraction without any monitoring. Stress trigger applies.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (private land); T1.2=0 (individual subsidy, no access right); T1.3~0.02 (negligible commons expenditure); T1.4=0 (equipment is individual asset)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.50 (locality fraction: dispersed beneficiaries); T2.2: SC/ST priority absent; T2.3=0
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (no livelihood output mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.1,000-2,000/year (labor savings); T3.3=0; T3.4=0

upgradePathway: Custom Hiring Center Commons Model: Transform individual equipment subsidies into gram-level custom hiring centers owned by gram cooperative — village retains equipment as commons asset
Commons Governance: Gram committee manages equipment allocation, maintenance schedules, and revenue distribution
Sustainability Lock: Mandatory 10% of hiring revenue to equipment O&M fund; annual maintenance audit condition for continued subsidy
Income Threshold: Target ₹5,000+/HH/year for equipment-dependent farmer households through cooperative hiring access

references.title: Sub-Mission on Agricultural Mechanization (SMAM) — Central Guidelines, MoAFW
Krishi Yantrikikarana — CG State Budget Component 2023-24

schemes.name: Sukhasma Bhandar Paddhati
schemes.reach: Small farmer and producer groups in grain-surplus districts
schemes.objective: Provide subsidized warehouse and cold storage infrastructure for small farmers and producer groups, enabling post-harvest preservation and value-addition for agricultural products.
governanceFlags: MISSING: T1.5 — No indigenous commons recognition; purely private storage subsidy
MISSING: T2.4 — No gram sabha deliberation on storage allocation or commons linkage
MISSING: T4.6 — No community-driven governance mechanism
CLIMATE-RISK: T4.7 — Groundwater stress trigger active

T1_1.note: Storage infrastructure is private facility (individual farmer or cooperative-owned commercial structure); no commons resource involved. Subsidy creates private asset.

T1_2.note: No community access right to storage; individual farmer or FPC owns and controls access to subsidized storage facility; no open-access or commons entitlement.

T1_3.note: Limited commons expenditure (~10%); scheme primarily subsidizes private storage infrastructure with minor spillover benefit to collective storage demonstration sites.

T1_4.note: Cold storage and warehouse are excludable private infrastructure; access is controlled by owner/operator; no community benefit-sharing requirement.

T1_5.flag: No indigenous commons recognition; purely private storage subsidy
T1_5.note: Scheme has no connection to traditional commons grain storage practices (community granaries) common in CG tribal culture.

T1_6.note: No legal mandate for commons orientation in storage; scheme guidelines focus on private cold storage subsidy without commons governance requirement.

T2_1.note: Locality fraction approximately 0.65; beneficiaries in grain-surplus districts but access concentrated among farmers with capital for storage co-investment. Below 0.70 threshold.

T2_2.note: SC/ST share approximately 20%; no explicit SC/ST priority in storage subsidy guidelines; capital co-investment requirement disadvantages marginal tribal households. Below 0.30 threshold.

T2_3.note: No gram sabha mandate; storage subsidy allocated through individual application at district level without community governance role.

T2_4.flag: No gram sabha deliberation on storage allocation or commons linkage
T2_4.note: Gram sabha has no role in storage infrastructure decisions; no community commons dimension to storage subsidy allocation.

T2_5.flag: CG GP Act commons provisions not activated
T2_5.note: No linkage to gram panchayat commons provisions; private storage facility not within panchayat commons governance framework.

T3_1.note: Income pathway mandated through storage-enabled value-addition; scheme design explicitly targets reducing post-harvest loss and capturing price premium through delayed sale — direct income mandate.

T3_2.note: Projected income increment ₹5,000–8,000/HH/year from post-harvest price arbitrage and reduced spoilage; midpoint ₹6,500 above the ₹5,000 projected income threshold.

pathways: Post-harvest price arbitrage (delayed sale at higher off-season price)
Reduced post-harvest loss (avoided income loss from spoilage)
T3_3.note: Single primary livelihood pathway (storage-enabled price premium); reduced loss functions as secondary benefit rather than distinct income pathway.

T3_4.offtakeMechanism: Market linkage through aggregator networks and state MSP procurement; partial linkage to CG Food Corporation procurement
T3_4.note: Partial institutional offtake through state MSP and aggregator network; storage enables price negotiation but guaranteed procurement is incomplete. Mapped to score 1 (functional partial mechanism).

T3_5.flag: No per-beneficiary income outcome tracking linking storage use to income change
T3_5.note: Monitoring tracks storage utilization rates and infrastructure status; no household-level income outcome attribution from storage value-addition.

T4_1.note: No replenishment rule for any natural resource; storage scheme has no sustainability conditionality for groundwater, soil, or commons assets. T4 entirely absent from scheme design.

T4_2.note: No extraction/replenishment framework applicable; storage scheme does not engage any subtractable commons resource; rho undefined and irrelevant.

T4_3.note: No monitoring framework of any kind for natural resources. CG stress threshold requires ≥2; scheme scores 0. Storage scheme has no ecological monitoring dimension.

T4_4.note: No penalty mechanism for resource degradation or non-compliance; storage subsidy disbursed without any sustainability conditionality.

T4_5.note: No O&M fund for storage infrastructure maintenance; no mandatory maintenance allocation from scheme budget.

T4_6.note: No community institution involved; individual or FPC ownership model with no community accountability or commons oversight structure.

T4_7.note: CG 70% blocks groundwater stressed. Stress trigger applies at state level; storage scheme does not address groundwater but stress threshold elevates T4.3 requirement across all CG agriculture schemes.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (private storage facility); T1.2=0 (no community access right); T1.3~0.10 (limited commons spend); T1.4=0 (excludable storage)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.65 (locality fraction); T2.2: SC/ST priority absent; T2.3=0 (no gram sabha mandate)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (income pathway through storage-enabled value addition); Delta-Y: Rs.5,000-8,000/HH/year; T3.3=1 (single pathway); T3.4=0.5 (partial offtake)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0 (no replenishment rule); T4.2: N/A; T4.3=0; T4.4=0; T4.5=0

upgradePathway: Commons Storage Model: Establish gram-level community storage facilities (infrastructure remains gram sabha property) — estimated cost ₹2-3 Cr
Commons Ownership: All farmers in the gram eligible for discounted storage rates; surplus revenue distributed to gram commons fund
Value-Addition Commons: Designate % of storage revenue to community processing infrastructure maintenance
Benefit-Sharing: Annual commons dividend from storage cooperative profits distributed to gram sabha members

references.title: Sukhasma Bhandar Paddhati — Scheme Guidelines, Agriculture Dept CG
Post-Harvest Infrastructure Development — CG Agriculture Budget Note 2024-25

schemes.name: Rainfed Area Development
schemes.nodalMinistry: Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare, GoI / Agriculture Dept CG (RKVY-NMAET component)
schemes.reach: 8 lakh+ hectares rainfed area; 12 priority rainfed districts of CG
schemes.objective: Promote sustainable rainfed agriculture through water harvesting commons (check-dams, johads, ponds), soil conservation, and crop diversification adapted to drought-prone regions with community-based natural resource management.

T1_1.note: Scheme explicitly targets community watershed commons: check-dams, johads, and farm ponds built on village commons land; water harvesting structures are gram sabha commons assets.

T1_2.note: Statutory community access right established under RKVY guidelines and CG Watershed Management Authority; community access to watershed commons codified in scheme legal framework.

T1_3.note: ≥80% of scheme expenditure on commons water harvesting infrastructure (check-dams, johads, ponds, contour bunds); well above 50% threshold. Remaining 20% on individual crop diversification support linked to commons water access.

T1_4.note: Groundwater recharge and watershed moisture benefits are non-excludable for entire watershed community; check-dam water is a village commons resource accessible to all gram panchayat residents.

T1_5.note: Scheme integrates traditional johad/naala-based water harvesting practices of CG rainfed communities; indigenous watershed knowledge incorporated in site selection and design guidelines.

T1_6.note: RKVY-NMAET Act and guidelines mandate community watershed governance committees with statutory authority; CG Watershed Development Mission provides state legal framework.

T2_1.note: Locality fraction approximately 0.88; scheme targets 12 priority rainfed districts with high concentration of rain-dependent farming communities; watershed unit ensures high geographic concentration.

T2_2.note: SC/ST share approximately 48%; scheme prioritizes tribal and scheduled caste rain-dependent farmers in CG's rainfed belt (Bastar, Surguja, Kanker districts); explicit ST priority in watershed site selection.

T2_3.note: Gram sabha governance mandated in RKVY watershed guidelines; Gram Water Committees are legally constituted with responsibility for commons water allocation, maintenance, and monitoring.

T2_4.note: Gram sabha formally deliberates on watershed development plans, water allocation rules, and annual maintenance priorities; gram water committee minutes are compliance requirement for fund release.

T2_5.note: CG GP Act commons provisions fully activated; gram panchayat has statutory authority over watershed commons assets under CG Panchayati Raj Act and watershed program MoU with state government.

T3_1.note: Livelihood output mandate explicit: scheme requires documented income improvement from watershed commons access as condition for phase-II funding release; RKVY MIS tracks income outcomes.

T3_2.note: Observed income increment ₹7,000–9,000/HH/year from combined groundwater access improvement (reduced extraction cost), enhanced crop yield, and horticulture from micro-watershed; midpoint ₹8,000 well above ₹3,000 threshold.

pathways: Groundwater commons access enabling irrigated crop production
Enhanced rainfed crop yield from soil moisture conservation
T3_3.note: Three or more distinct livelihood income pathways from commons watershed; groundwater, soil moisture, and horticulture are independently measurable income streams.

T3_4.offtakeMechanism: NABARD watershed cooperative linkage + CG Agriculture cooperative procurement; horticulture sold through CGMF market linkage
T3_4.note: Institutional offtake guaranteed through NABARD watershed cooperative program linkage and CG government procurement channel for certified watershed produce.

T3_5.note: RKVY-NMAET MIS tracks per-watershed household income improvement; annual outcome audit required for phase-II funding; outcome monitoring is built into scheme compliance structure.

T4_1.rule: E/R ≤1.2 groundwater extraction rule; shortfall triggers mandatory dry-season recharge contribution from scheme-supported groups (RKVY Watershed Guidelines, Section 7.3)
T4_1.note: Binding replenishment rule codified in RKVY watershed guidelines: E/R ratio must be maintained at ≤1.2; watershed committee must demonstrate seasonal recharge exceeds extraction shortfall.

T4_2.note: Estimated average E/R ratio ~1.15 across implementing watershed sites based on CGWB block-level data and scheme groundwater monitoring reports; within ≤1.2 rule limit.

metrics: Annual crop yield per ha in watershed area
T4_3.note: Bi-annual monitoring (pre and post-monsoon) with annual watershed audit as compliance condition; outcome-based metrics tracked at watershed level. Score 2 meets CG stress threshold of ≥2.

T4_4.note: Fund withholding penalty mechanism active: 15% of annual scheme allocation withheld if watershed monitoring shows E/R >1.2 or pasture degradation >10%; penalty released on restoration evidence.

T4_5.note: Mandatory O&M fund: 10% of scheme capital allocated as dedicated maintenance reserve for check-dam and watershed structure maintenance; held in gram water committee cooperative account.

T4_6.note: Gram Water Committee (GWC) is a community-driven institution with statutory authority, annual general meetings, elected leadership, and financial accountability for watershed commons management.

T4_7.note: CG 70% blocks groundwater stressed; this scheme directly addresses the stress through watershed recharge. T4.3 threshold of ≥2 is met (score 2), qualifying scheme as climate-responsive SOC.

evidence.t1: T1.1=B (community commons: check-dams/johads on village land); T1.2=1 (statutory community access right); T1.3~0.80 (≥80% expenditure on commons water harvesting); T1.4=2 (non-excludable benefit)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.88 (locality fraction); T2.2~0.48 (SC/ST share); T2.3=1 (gram sabha governance mandated)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (livelihood output mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.7,000-9,000/HH/year; T3.3=3 (multiple pathways: groundwater/crops/horticulture); T3.4=1 (institutional offtake guaranteed)
evidence.t4: T4.1=1 (replenishment rule codified); T4.2~1.15 (E/R ≤1.2 maintained); T4.3=2 (bi-annual monitoring + annual audit); T4.4=1 (fund withholding penalty); T4.5=1 (O&M fund 10% of capital)

upgradePathway: Climate Finance Integration: Link water harvesting commons to carbon credit revenue (NTFP + watershed carbon sequestration)
Biodiversity Commons: Integrate millet/pulse diversity conservation with livelihood benefit; seed sovereignty mandate
Micro-Watershed Certification: Achieve international commons certification for gram watershed commons (connects to global sustainable agriculture premium markets)

references.title: RKVY-NMAET — Rainfed Area Development Sub-component Guidelines, MoAFW
CGWB — Groundwater Year Book Chhattisgarh; Block-Level Stress and Watershed Recharge Data
NABARD Watershed Programme — Impact Evaluation, Chhattisgarh Component

schemes.name: Sub-mission Agri-mechanization
schemes.nodalMinistry: Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare, GoI / Agriculture Dept CG
schemes.reach: Small and marginal farmers, Custom Hiring Center operators, CG
schemes.objective: Promote custom hiring centers and equipment pooling for small farmers, reducing individual machinery investment burden while improving mechanization access through shared equipment model.
governanceFlags: MISSING: T1.5 — No indigenous commons recognition; CHC model is private operator not community commons
MISSING: T2.4 — No gram sabha involvement in CHC governance
MISSING: T3.5 — No systematic income outcome tracking from equipment access
MISSING: T4.6 — No community-driven governance; CHC is private enterprise
CLIMATE-RISK: T4.7 — Groundwater stress trigger active

T1_1.note: Custom Hiring Centers (CHC) are private enterprises subsidized by government; equipment ownership and control vest in private CHC operator, not in gram commons. Despite 'shared access' intent, governance is private-commercial not commons.

T1_2.note: No community access right established; access to CHC equipment is conditional on commercial hiring fee paid to private operator; no commons entitlement or gram sabha authority.

T1_3.note: Approximately 20% of scheme expenditure directed toward shared access infrastructure; remainder subsidizes private CHC operator capital investment. Below 50% commons threshold.

T1_4.note: CHC equipment is formally accessible to all farmers in the area at hire rates (partial non-excludability); however, commercial hire fee creates effective exclusion for poorest households. Score 1 for partial.

T1_5.flag: No indigenous commons recognition; CHC model is private operator not community commons
T1_5.note: CHC model is a commercial-franchise model, not grounded in traditional shared-equipment practices; ignores existing community tool-sharing customs in CG villages.

T1_6.note: SMAM guidelines authorize CHC as private entity subsidy without commons governance mandate; no legal requirement for community ownership or gram sabha oversight of CHC.

T2_1.note: Locality fraction approximately 0.70; CHC sites are in agricultural villages but access is geographically bounded by hire fee affordability; borderline on locality threshold, fails on T2_2.

T2_2.note: SC/ST share approximately 20%; no explicit SC/ST priority in CHC subsidy guidelines; commercial hire fee model disadvantages SC/ST households with lower cash liquidity. Below 0.30 threshold.

T2_3.note: No gram sabha mandate; CHC licensed through district agriculture office as private enterprise without community governance role or gram sabha authority over equipment access.

T2_4.flag: No gram sabha involvement in CHC governance
T2_4.note: Gram sabha has no role in CHC site selection, pricing, equipment allocation, or operator accountability; purely private commercial arrangement.

T2_5.flag: CG GP Act commons provisions not activated
T2_5.note: No linkage to gram panchayat commons provisions; CHC operates outside panchayat commons governance framework as private licensee.

T3_1.note: No mandated livelihood output; CHC provides equipment access at commercial rate without any income mandate, productivity guarantee, or livelihood outcome commitment for using farmers.

T3_2.note: Projected labor savings ₹500–1,000/HH/year from equipment access; midpoint ₹750 far below ₹3,000 observed and ₹5,000 projected thresholds. Benefits are marginal labor-cost reductions.

T3_3.note: Single equipment-access channel; no diversified livelihood pathways; benefit is limited to reduced mechanization cost for individual farm operations.

T3_4.note: No institutional offtake mechanism; CHC scheme does not create any market linkage or value chain integration for farm produce.

T3_5.flag: No systematic income outcome tracking from equipment access
T3_5.note: Monitoring tracks CHC registrations and equipment counts only; no income benefit measurement per household from mechanization access.

T4_1.note: No replenishment rule; mechanization subsidy has no sustainability conditionality for soil, water, or natural resources. No ecological obligation attached to CHC operation or subsidy.

T4_2.note: No extraction/replenishment framework; CHC does not engage any directly measurable commons resource; rho undefined and not applicable to current scheme design.

T4_3.note: No monitoring framework of any kind. CG stress threshold requires ≥2; CHC scheme scores 0 on all resource monitoring metrics.

T4_4.note: No penalty mechanism; CHC subsidy disbursed without sustainability conditionality or ecological performance requirements.

T4_5.note: No O&M fund for CHC equipment maintenance; maintenance is private operator responsibility with no scheme-mandated maintenance reserve.

T4_6.flag: No community-driven governance; CHC is private enterprise
T4_6.note: CHC is a private commercial entity; no community institution governs equipment access, pricing, or sustainability; accountability is to government licensor not to commons users.

T4_7.note: CG 70% blocks groundwater stressed. Mechanization of groundwater pumping (submersible, centrifugal pumps) via CHC can accelerate extraction; stress trigger applies, elevating T4.3 threshold.

evidence.t1: T1.1=D (shared but private-entity-owned equipment); T1.2=0.5 (partial access right); T1.3~0.20 (some commons expenditure); T1.4=1 (partial non-excludability)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.70 (locality fraction); T2.2: SC/ST priority absent; T2.3=0 (no gram sabha mandate)
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (no income indicator mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.500-1,000/year (minimal savings); T3.3=0; T3.4=0

upgradePathway: Transform to Gram Commons Equipment Pool: Equipment pool managed by gram cooperative; all village farmers eligible for usage
Gram Sabha Governance: Equipment allocation decided by commons committee with SC/ST priority access
O&M Fund: 10% of hiring revenue to mandatory equipment maintenance fund; annual audit
Income Mandate: Minimum ₹5,000/HH/year labor savings through equipment access; track and report annually

references.title: Sub-Mission on Agricultural Mechanization (SMAM) — Operational Guidelines 2023, MoAFW
Sub-Mission Agri-Mechanization — CG Component Budget 2023-24

schemes.name: PM-KISAN (Chhattisgarh Component)
schemes.nodalMinistry: Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare, Government of India
schemes.reach: 25+ lakh registered landholding farmer families in Chhattisgarh
schemes.objective: Provide direct income support (₹6,000/year in 3 installments) to all landholding farmers regardless of productivity, ensuring household income floor and food security.
governanceFlags: MISSING: T1.5 — No indigenous commons recognition; DBT scheme with no commons dimension
MISSING: T2.3 — No gram sabha mandate; bypasses community governance entirely
MISSING: T2.4 — No gram sabha commons deliberation
MISSING: T3.5 — No commons-linked outcome tracking
MISSING: T4.6 — No community-driven governance mechanism
CLIMATE-RISK: T4.7 — Groundwater stress trigger active

T1_1.note: DBT scheme explicitly for private landholding farmers; no commons resource involved in design, eligibility, or delivery. Land records (khata) confirm private ownership as eligibility criterion.

T1_2.note: No community access right; cash transfer is a purely individual entitlement based on land ownership records with no commons governance or collective resource dimension.

T1_3.note: Zero expenditure on commons; 100% of scheme budget is direct bank-to-farmer DBT with no commons infrastructure or collective resource investment component whatsoever.

T1_4.note: Cash transfer is a fully excludable private benefit; non-landholders, sharecroppers, and landless laborers are explicitly excluded; no community benefit or non-excludable resource created.

T1_5.flag: No indigenous commons recognition; DBT scheme with no commons dimension
T1_5.note: PM-KISAN has no connection to traditional commons institutions, customary land rights, or indigenous farming commons practices of any kind.

T1_6.note: PM-KISAN Act explicitly targets private landholders; no mandate for commons resource targeting in scheme legislation or operational guidelines.

T2_1.note: Locality fraction approximately 0.75; scheme targets village-resident landholding farmers with registration by gram panchayat village; reasonably high geographic concentration in agricultural communities.

T2_2.note: SC/ST share approximately 50% of CG beneficiaries, reflecting state demographic distribution (32% ST, 12%+ SC); universal coverage of all landholders naturally includes high SC/ST representation.

T2_3.note: No gram sabha mandate; DBT delivered directly to individual farmer bank accounts through Aadhaar-linked system — bypasses community governance entirely.

T2_4.note: Gram sabha has no role in PM-KISAN targeting, disbursement, or accountability; scheme is fully centralized with no community governance dimension.

T2_5.flag: CG GP Act commons provisions not activated
T2_5.note: No linkage to CG Gram Panchayat Act commons management; gram panchayat role limited to data verification of land records only.

T3_1.note: No mandated livelihood output from commons; ₹6,000 cash transfer is an income floor subsidy, not a commons-based livelihood mandate; scheme does not require productive investment of transfer.

T3_2.note: Fixed observed transfer ₹6,000/year; although numerically above ₹3,000 threshold, T3.2 pass requires income to be commons-derived or commons-linked. This is unconditional cash transfer, not commons income increment — T3_1 failure disqualifies T3 regardless.

T3_3.note: Single cash transfer channel with no livelihood pathway diversification; transfer does not create or support any productive commons engagement.

T3_4.note: No institutional offtake; DBT does not create any market linkage, value chain, or institutional procurement mechanism for farmer output.

T3_5.note: PM-KISAN MIS tracks DBT disbursement and farmer registration only; no tracking of productive investment, commons engagement, or income improvement from the transfer.

T4_1.note: No replenishment rule; DBT scheme has no sustainability conditionality for any natural resource; no ecological obligation or conservation requirement attached to cash transfer.

T4_2.note: No extraction/replenishment framework; DBT scheme does not engage any subtractable commons resource; rho undefined and not applicable.

T4_3.note: No resource monitoring framework. CG stress threshold requires ≥2; DBT scheme scores 0. Transfer continuity is unconditional with no ecological performance linkage.

T4_4.note: No penalty mechanism; PM-KISAN disbursed unconditionally regardless of farmer resource use behavior or ecological outcomes.

T4_5.note: No O&M fund or stewardship mechanism; cash transfer creates no lasting resource asset requiring maintenance.

T4_6.note: No community institution involved in PM-KISAN governance; fully centralized digital DBT with no community participation, accountability, or oversight.

T4_7.note: CG 70% blocks groundwater stressed. PM-KISAN cash may be invested in tube-well deepening or pump purchase, accelerating extraction; stress trigger applies at state level elevating T4.3 threshold.

evidence.t1: T1.1=C (private landholding); T1.2=0 (no commons resource involved); T1.3~0.00 (no expenditure on commons); T1.4=0
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.75 (locality fraction); T2.2~0.50 (SC/ST share); T2.3=0 (no gram sabha mandate)
evidence.t3: T3.1=0 (income transfer, not livelihood mandate); Delta-Y: Rs.6,000 (income transfer only, not commons-based); T3.3=0; T3.4=0

upgradePathway: Link PM-KISAN to Commons Participation: Condition transfer on farmer participation in gram-level commons committee (Atal Jal model: participation linked to water budgeting)
Livelihood Productivity Linkage: Offer top-up transfer for commons-based productivity investment (e.g., ₹2,000 bonus for joining water user group)
Commons Governance Mandate: Require gram sabha establishment of farmer commons committee as prerequisite for block-level disbursement
Sustainability Monitoring: Link future transfer disbursement to gram-level groundwater sustainability metrics (E/R ≤1.2)

references.title: PM-KISAN — Official Portal, Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare
PM-KISAN Beneficiary Data — Chhattisgarh State, 2023-24 Disbursement Statistics

schemes.name: National Food Security Mission
schemes.nodalMinistry: Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare, GoI / Agriculture Dept CG
schemes.reach: Targeted rainfed and semi-arid districts; pulse and oilseed belt of CG
schemes.objective: Increase production of pulses and oilseeds through technology dissemination, varietal improvement, and crop insurance, strengthening food security and farmer incomes in rain-fed regions.
governanceFlags: STRUCTURAL: T4.1 — No mandatory crop rotation replenishment rule for soil nitrogen
STRUCTURAL: T4.3 — No soil health or commons monitoring framework; score 0 on all monitoring metrics
MISSING: T4.6 — No community-driven commons governance mechanism for seed commons
CLIMATE-RISK: T4.7 — Groundwater stress trigger active; soil-water commons under pressure

T1_1.note: Scheme targets rainfed watershed commons as the shared resource base; pulse/oilseed cultivation on community-managed rainfed watersheds and soil commons; seed banks are community commons resource.

T1_2.note: Community access right to rainfed watershed commons established through gram-level seed production groups; access formalized but not statutory under a central commons act.

T1_3.note: Approximately 70% of scheme expenditure directed toward commons watershed activities: seed bank establishment, demonstration plots on commons land, soil health interventions; above 50% threshold.

T1_4.note: Partial non-excludability: watershed soil moisture and groundwater recharge benefits from pulse cultivation (nitrogen fixation, reduced runoff) are shared across gram panchayat; seed bank accessible to registered members.

T1_5.note: Scheme integrates traditional pulse and oilseed varieties of CG rainfed commons; indigenous seed diversity conservation is an explicit component of NFSM seed bank design.

T1_6.note: NFSM operational guidelines mandate community seed production groups with formal constitution; CG state implements through Krishi Vigyan Kendras and gram-level commons seed banks.

T2_1.note: Locality fraction approximately 0.82; scheme targets rainfed farming communities in identified districts with high spatial concentration; watershed as unit ensures geographic clustering.

T2_2.note: SC/ST share approximately 45%; NFSM explicitly targets pulse/oilseed growing communities in CG's tribal belt (Bastar, Surguja, Kanker); above 0.30 threshold.

T2_3.note: Community governance body mandated: gram-level seed production groups constituted with accountability to gram sabha; group decisions on seed variety selection and distribution are formalized.

T2_4.note: Gram sabha deliberates on seed bank governance, variety selection, and commons watershed development priorities within NFSM; documented in gram sabha records at implementation sites.

T2_5.note: CG GP Act provisions activated for seed bank commons management; gram panchayat has authority over seed bank infrastructure under state agriculture commons framework.

T3_1.note: Income mandate explicit: NFSM scheme tracks pulse/oilseed production improvement and household income enhancement as primary performance indicator; income outcome is reported in MIS.

T3_2.note: Observed income increment ₹5,500–7,000/HH/year from pulse and oilseed yield improvement and price realization; midpoint ₹6,250 well above ₹3,000 observed threshold.

pathways: Nitrogen fixation reducing fertilizer cost on subsequent crops
T3_3.note: Two primary livelihood pathways (pulse + oilseed income) plus indirect nitrogen-fixation cost-saving pathway; minimum two distinct measurable income streams.

T3_4.offtakeMechanism: Pulse procurement at MSP through CG State Civil Supplies Corporation; Oilseed procurement through National Cooperative Export Limited (NCEL)
T3_4.note: Institutional offtake guaranteed through state MSP procurement mechanism for both pulses and oilseeds; CG state implements NFSM with guaranteed procurement as design requirement.

T3_5.flag: No systematic per-household outcome tracking separating NFSM impact from non-scheme production
T3_5.note: NFSM MIS tracks area under pulse/oilseed and production totals; household-level income attribution specific to NFSM intervention (vs. baseline) is not systematically measured.

T4_1.note: No mandatory crop rotation replenishment rule codified; pulse cultivation improves soil nitrogen in practice but no minimum pulse area mandate per farm or gram is binding. Partial practice without enforceable rule — score 0.

T4_2.note: Soil nitrogen extraction/replenishment ratio undefined and unmeasured; no soil nitrogen monitoring framework linked to scheme compliance or fund release conditions.

T4_3.note: No soil health or commons monitoring framework of any kind; scheme tracks area and production but not soil quality, nitrogen content, or organic matter indicators. CG stress requires ≥2; score is 0.

T4_4.note: No penalty mechanism for soil degradation or non-compliance with any sustainability standard; scheme benefits disbursed unconditionally.

T4_5.note: No O&M fund for seed bank maintenance or commons soil health investment; no mandatory maintenance allocation from scheme budget.

T4_6.flag: No community-driven commons governance mechanism for seed commons sustainability
T4_6.note: Seed production groups are constituted but lack genuine commons governance authority; group decisions are advisory and not linked to scheme fund conditionality or ecological monitoring.

T4_7.note: CG 70% blocks groundwater stressed; pulse/oilseed rainfed commons are directly exposed to water stress. Stress trigger elevates T4.3 threshold to ≥2 for outcome monitoring of water-soil commons.

evidence.t1: T1.1=B (rainfed commons watersheds); T1.2=1 (community access right); T1.3~0.70 (expenditure on commons); T1.4=1 (partial non-excludability)
evidence.t2: T2.1~0.82 (locality fraction); T2.2~0.45 (SC/ST share); T2.3=1 (community governance body mandated)
evidence.t3: T3.1=1 (income indicator mandated); Delta-Y: Rs.5,500-7,000/HH/year; T3.3=2 (multiple pathways: pulses/oilseeds); T3.4=1 (institutional offtake)
evidence.t4: T4.1=0.5 (partial mandate); T4.2: undefined; T4.3=0 (no monitoring framework); T4.4=0; T4.5=0.5

upgradePathway: Mandatory Crop Rotation Rule: Codify ≥30% annual pulse/oilseed cultivation mandate per gram as condition for subsidy (soil nitrogen replenishment model)
Soil Nitrogen Monitoring: Establish soil health testing for nitrogen content and organic matter; link to subsidy eligibility
Legume Restoration Target: Set annual pulse cultivation area target with subsidy conditionality — estimated cost ₹2-3 Cr
O&M Fund for Seed Production: Earmark 8% of NFSM capital as dedicated maintenance fund for seed banks and demonstration plots; timeline 12-18 months
